title: When the System You Built Is Used Wrong
pressure: The integrity system has been handed off, and another leader is now using it. But the system is being applied in a way that looks compliant while creating fear, rigidity, or selective pressure. People are watching whether you take control back, protect the successor, or build correction into the system itself.
tradeoff: Correct the misuse personally, or create a correction mechanism that keeps ownership distributed.

incident.roleShift: from system inheritance designer to system correction owner

propagation.exitEffect: The system becomes dependent on your personal correction or the successor’s unchecked interpretation.
propagation.entryEffect: The system gains a correction loop that does not require ownership to return to you.
propagation.reExposureNote: The next misuse will reveal whether correction became a system behavior or a leadership rescue.

action_contract.description: Respond to a distorted use of the integrity system by creating one observable correction loop that keeps ownership with the operating system, not with personal authority.

AIR_logic.success: The system can identify distortion, correct application, and preserve distributed ownership.
AIR_logic.miss: The correction either recentralizes control or allows the distorted version to become the new norm.

forced_reset.trigger: A staff member says the integrity system now feels like a weapon instead of a standard.
forced_reset.action: Separate the standard from the distorted application, name the correction pathway, and require a documented re-check.

pattern_detection.recentralized_control: Takes the decision back personally whenever the system is misused.
pattern_detection.stability_preservation: Avoids naming distortion because the successor is still new.
pattern_detection.successor_protection: Protects the new leader’s authority at the cost of system feedback.
pattern_detection.symbolic_correction: Fixes one case without changing how future distortion is detected.

A.escalation_text: After you personally correct the decision, staff feel relieved, but the successor stops making independent calls and starts waiting for your interpretation on hard cases.
A.pressure_increase: The correction protected the case but weakened system inheritance.

action_decision.prompt: What will you do within 72 hours to correct the misuse without taking the system back?
action_decision.context: The case was corrected, but the successor now sees you as the final interpreter again.

choices.label: Within 72 hours, assign the successor and one peer leader to re-review the corrected case against the written criteria and document the revised application without you making the final call.
Continue reviewing difficult integrity cases personally until the successor shows stronger judgment.

second_choices.label: Stay involved until the successor becomes more consistent
Move correction back into a defined review process owned by the successor and one peer leader
second_choices.cost: Requires tolerating imperfect correction without taking ownership back.

B.escalation_text: Because the distortion was not named, another office copies the successor’s rigid application and staff begin avoiding the integrity pathway unless they feel protected.
B.pressure_increase: A learning gap has become a replicated operating behavior.

action_decision.prompt: What will you do within 72 hours to stop the distorted version from becoming the standard?
action_decision.context: The system is now being copied incorrectly because no one named the difference between the standard and the distortion.

choices.label: Within 72 hours, publish a correction note that defines the specific application error, separates it from the successor personally, and states the correct criteria for future cases.
Send additional examples to leaders and ask them to use better judgment in future cases.

second_choices.label: Give leaders more examples so they understand the intended use
second_choices.cost: Adds explanation but still avoids naming the current distortion.

second_choices.label: Name the distortion as a system application error, not a successor failure
second_choices.cost: Creates discomfort but prevents the wrong version from spreading.

C.escalation_text: Quiet feedback protects the successor, but staff conclude that system feedback only travels upward if leadership approves it first.
C.pressure_increase: The correction path now looks permission-based instead of system-based.

action_decision.prompt: What will you do within 72 hours to make system feedback independent of leader comfort?
action_decision.context: If feedback needs permission, the system cannot correct itself under pressure.

choices.label: Within 72 hours, create a system-application feedback loop where staff can flag distorted use against defined criteria and receive documented review from two leaders.
Continue collecting feedback privately and decide which concerns should be shared with the successor.

second_choices.label: Keep feedback quiet until the successor has more confidence
second_choices.cost: Protects the leader while teaching staff that feedback is conditional.

second_choices.label: Create a feedback loop that reviews system application without requiring personal approval
second_choices.cost: Reduces leadership control over what gets surfaced.

D.escalation_text: Once the correction pathway is announced, a partner doctor says the system is becoming too bureaucratic and that leaders will stop making timely decisions if every application can be challenged.
D.pressure_increase: Self-correction now threatens speed, authority comfort, and leadership discretion.

action_decision.prompt: What will you do within 72 hours to keep self-correction real without making the system unusable?
action_decision.context: The correction pathway must be narrow enough to operate and strong enough to challenge distorted authority.

choices.label: Within 72 hours, publish three narrow correction triggers: criteria mismatch, relationship-based exception, and authority-based suppression, with a required two-leader documented review.
Restrict correction requests to cases that senior leadership first agrees are serious enough to review.

second_choices.label: Limit the correction pathway to rare cases approved by senior leadership
second_choices.cost: Makes the pathway safer but weakens self-correction.

second_choices.label: Define narrow criteria for correction while preserving the right to challenge distorted application
second_choices.cost: Requires leaders to accept structured challenge without turning every disagreement into review.

ending_identity_choice.prompt: When the system is used wrong, what kind of integrity are you choosing?

choices.label: Integrity that depends on the original leader to rescue distorted application
Integrity that can identify, challenge, and correct its own distortion
Step in and personally correct the misused decision
choices.subtext: It feels necessary because the system may harm trust if the wrong application continues.

choices.label: Avoid calling it misuse yet because the successor is still learning
choices.subtext: It feels stabilizing because naming the distortion too early may weaken the successor.

choices.label: Protect the successor’s authority and handle feedback quietly
choices.subtext: It feels respectful because public correction may make the handoff look failed.

choices.label: Create a correction pathway for distorted use of the system
choices.subtext: It costs comfort because it allows staff to challenge not only decisions, but also how the system itself is being applied.
